source_book: NCERT Class 11 Physics Part I Ch.8 + HC Verma Ch.5

real_world_anchor.primary: Three blocks stacked on each other. Showing every force in one picture is chaos. A free body diagram says: look at one body at a time.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A ladder leaning on a wall. Drawing every force at once confuses even experts. Isolate the ladder and only its own forces remain.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A JEE problem with five bodies connected together. Writing Newton's law is impossible without a free body diagram first.

constraints: Isolate EXACTLY ONE body (or one sub-system). Never two bodies together.
Show ALL forces on that body — every single one, no exceptions.
Weight (mg) always acts downward through the centre of mass.
Every removed contact or connection becomes one force arrow.
Do NOT show forces the body exerts on others — only forces ON the body.
Label every force with its symbol and direction.
After the FBD, write sum(Fx) = m*ax and sum(Fy) = m*ay and solve.
fbd_rules: Rule 1: Isolate EXACTLY ONE body (or sub-system). Never two bodies together.
Rule 2: Show ALL forces on that body — every single one, no exceptions.
Rule 3: Weight (mg) always acts downward through the center of mass.
Rule 4: Every removed contact or connection becomes a force arrow.
Rule 5: Do NOT show forces that the body exerts on others — only forces ON the body.
Rule 6: Label every force with its symbol and direction.
force_checklist: Weight (mg) — always present if the body has mass
Normal reaction (N) — from every surface in contact
Friction (f) — from every rough surface in contact
Tension (T) — from every string or rope attached
Hinge force (H, V) — if a pin joint is present
Applied force (F) — any external push or pull
Spring force (kx) — if a spring is attached

STATE_1.title: The Problem: A Complex System — Where Do You Even Start?

scene_composition.text: Three stacked blocks. Which forces act where? Which equation applies to which body?
Without an FBD: chaos.  With an FBD: one body, one equation at a time.

tts_sentences.text_en: Three blocks stacked on each other. You are asked to find the forces everywhere.
Trying to draw everything in one picture gets confusing fast. Which normal reaction belongs to which pair? Where does each weight act?
The free body diagram answers this by looking at only one body at a time. All other bodies are temporarily ignored.
This is a technique, not a trick. It is the first step in every single exam problem involving forces.

STATE_2.title: The Isolation — Cut the Body Free. Every Cut Becomes a Force.

scene_composition.text:
Remove A -> N1 arrow (A's force on B).
Remove C -> N2 arrow (C's force on B).
Each removed connection becomes one force arrow.

tts_sentences.text_en: Isolate block B. Remove A and remove C. Only B remains on the page.
When A is removed, the push A exerted on B becomes a force arrow, N one, pointing downward.
When C is removed, the push C exerted on B becomes a force arrow, N two, pointing upward.
This is the magic of the free body diagram. Every removed connection is replaced by a force arrow. Tap continue.

STATE_3.title: The WHY — The Body Only Feels Forces. It Does Not Know Its Neighbours.

scene_composition.text: Block B has no idea whether A is above or C is below. It only feels the forces acting on it — N1, N2, mg.

tts_sentences.text_en: Block B has no idea whether A is above or C is below. It only feels forces acting on its own surface.
N one is a downward force. N two is an upward force. The weight is downward.
Write Newton's second law directly: sum of forces in y equals N two minus N one minus mg equals zero.
The free body diagram turned a complicated system into a single clean equation. That is its real power.

STATE_4.title: FBD Step-by-Step — Book on a Flat Table (Simplest Case)

scene_composition.right_label: Free body diagram of the book

left_scene.text: Looks simple — what forces are here?

right_scene.text: Two forces. sum(Fy) = N - mg = 0. N = mg = 4.9 N.

scene_composition.text: The simplest template — memorise this pattern.
Every harder problem is just this pattern with more arrows added.

tts_sentences.text_en: The simplest free body diagram. A book resting on a table.
Isolate the book. Remove the table, and the contact becomes an upward normal reaction. Gravity still pulls straight down.
Two forces: N upward and mg downward. Sum of forces in y equals N minus mg equals zero, so N equals mg equals four point nine newtons.
This template repeats for every problem. Isolate the body, replace each contact with an arrow, apply Newton's law. Tap continue.

STATE_5.title: FBD on an Incline — Three Forces, Only mg Is Vertical

right_scene.text: mg vertical.  N perp surface.  f along surface.

scene_composition.text:
1. mg is ALWAYS vertical — never rotate with the incline.
2. N is ALWAYS perpendicular to the surface — rotates with it.
3. f is ALWAYS along the surface, opposing sliding.

tts_sentences.text_en: On an inclined surface, three forces live on the free body diagram — mg, N, and f.
mg is always straight down, even on an incline. Weight is a gravitational force and does not care about the surface.
N is always perpendicular to the surface. Tilt the incline and N tilts with it.
f always lies along the surface, pointing opposite to the sliding direction. These three rules repeat on every incline problem.

STATE_6.title: The Six-Force Checklist — Never Miss a Force Again

scene_composition.text:
Checklist:
- Weight (mg)
- N — every surface contact
- f — every rough surface
- T — every string/rope
- Spring (kx) — if attached
- Hinge (H, V) — if pin joint

scene_composition.equation: After the FBD: write sum(Fx) = m*ax and sum(Fy) = m*ay. Solve for the unknowns.

tts_sentences.text_en: Remember this six-force checklist and apply it mentally every time you build a free body diagram.
Weight is always present. Normal comes from every surface in contact. Friction comes from every rough surface. Tension comes from every string.
Spring force and hinge force appear only if those connections are present.
Miss a single force and your equation is wrong. A complete FBD is what makes Newton's law work.

panel_b_config.title: Force Balance Check
panel_b_config.subtitle: sum(Fx) and sum(Fy) must equal zero for equilibrium, or ma for acceleration

annotations.text: A bar at zero means that component is balanced.
A non-zero bar means net force, so the body accelerates.

regeneration_variants.label: Atwood Machine — Two FBDs
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Two masses on an Atwood machine. Draw a separate free body diagram for each mass. The same T appears in both diagrams.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Isolate each body and show all forces. T appears in both FBDs as the string force.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: FBD of m1: T up, m1*g down. FBD of m2: T up, m2*g down. The two T's are equal because the string is massless.

regeneration_variants.label: Block Hanging from Two Strings at Different Angles
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A block hangs from two strings attached to the ceiling at different angles. The FBD shows T1 and T2 pointing along their strings, plus mg straight down.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Isolate the block. Resolve T1 and T2 into x and y components. sum(Fx) = 0 and sum(Fy) = 0 give the two tensions.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Block body. String 1 going up-left at angle alpha. String 2 going up-right at angle beta. mg pointing straight down.

regeneration_variants.label: Person Standing in an Accelerating Lift
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A person stands on a spring scale inside a lift that accelerates upward at a m/s^2. Draw the person's FBD.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Isolate the person. Two forces: N from scale (up), mg (down). Apply sum(Fy) = ma -> N - mg = ma -> N = m(g + a). Apparent weight differs from true weight.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Person as rect inside lift. Scale below. N up, mg down, labelled 'lift accelerates upward at a'.

epic_c_branches.misconception: A free body diagram shows the forces on multiple bodies at once.
trigger_phrases: show A and B together in FBD
one FBD for the whole system

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: FBD Shows Forces on Multiple Bodies

scene_composition.right_label: Correct — one body at a time

left_scene.text:
Two bodies together.
Newton's law cannot be written this way
without a separate FBD of each.

right_scene.text: One body. sum(F) = ma can be written directly.

scene_composition.text: Rule: one FBD = exactly ONE body (or one chosen sub-system).

Internal action-reaction pairs between the two bodies
cancel each other — so a 'both bodies' FBD tells you nothing useful.

tts_sentences.text_en: Recall the definition of a free body diagram — one body or one sub-system, fully isolated.
If you draw two bodies together, you would need to show the action-reaction pair of internal forces between them.
Those internal forces cancel each other, so the diagram tells you nothing useful.
One FBD means one body. Three stacked blocks means three separate FBDs.

EPIC_C_2.title: One Body Per FBD — Then Solve the System

scene_composition.text: Each FBD yields one equation. Solve the equations together to find every unknown.

tts_sentences.text_en: Draw a separate FBD for each body — one for A, one for B, one for C.
Each FBD gives you one Newton's law equation.
Three bodies give three equations. Three unknowns like N one, N two, and N three get solved simultaneously.
This systematic approach scales. Whether you have two bodies or ten, the process is the same.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Gravity acts where the body is held or supported.
trigger_phrases: gravity acts at the support point

EPIC_C_1.title: Gravity Always Acts at the Centre of Mass — Nowhere Else

scene_composition.left_label: Belief: weight at the support point
scene_composition.right_label: Reality: weight at the centre of mass

left_scene.text:
Wrong. Gravity is not a contact force.
It acts through the centre of mass.

right_scene.text: Correct. mg acts at the centre of mass, always pointing down.

scene_composition.text: Gravity is a field force — it cares about mass, not contact.

Rule: always draw mg starting at the geometric centre
of a uniform body, pointing straight down.

tts_sentences.text_en: Gravity is a field force. It does not need contact to act.
That is why gravity acts on the centre of mass of a body, not on its support point or contact point.
Whether a table is under the block or not, mg still points straight down from the block's centre.
In every FBD, draw the mg arrow starting at the geometric centre of the body, pointing down.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The free body diagram only needs to show the weight.

EPIC_C_1.title: Missing N = Wrong Equation = Wrong Answer

left_scene.text:
sum(Fy) = -mg != 0.
Equation says the book accelerates down —
but it is sitting still. Contradiction.

right_scene.text: sum(Fy) = N - mg = 0. The book is stationary. Correct.

scene_composition.text: One missing force = one wrong answer.

Rule: every physical contact becomes exactly ONE normal reaction arrow.
Surface contact -> N perpendicular to that surface.

tts_sentences.text_en: If you show only mg, your equation says the net force is downward and the book should fall. But the book is not falling — something is wrong.
The missing force is N, the upward push of the table. That must always be shown in the diagram.
The rule is simple. Every physical contact becomes one normal reaction arrow. Table contact means N upward.
Missing a force means a wrong equation, which means a wrong answer. The FBD must be complete.

epic_c_branches.misconception: If the body pushes the table, the body's FBD should include that push on itself.
trigger_phrases: both newton third law forces on one body

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: Draw Both Third-Law Forces on the Same FBD

scene_composition.right_label: Correct — only forces ON the book

left_scene.text:
Equation would become nonsense —
sum(Fy) no longer equals zero.

right_scene.text: Only forces acting ON the book appear here.

scene_composition.text: Newton's 3rd law pairs live on DIFFERENT FBDs.

The book-on-table force acts on the TABLE, not on the book.
It belongs on the TABLE's FBD, never on the book's.

tts_sentences.text_en: Newton's third law says that if the book pushes the table, the table pushes the book back with equal force.
Those two forces are real, but they act on DIFFERENT bodies. One on the book, the other on the table.
The FBD of the book shows only the forces ON the book. The book-on-table force belongs on the TABLE's FBD, not the book's.
Mixing the pair up on a single FBD doubles the same physical effect and gives a nonsense equation.

EPIC_C_2.title: The Two-Body Rule — Each FBD Gets Exactly One Half of the Pair

scene_composition.text:
N on the book's FBD and N' on the table's FBD
are the third-law pair — equal, opposite, on DIFFERENT bodies.

tts_sentences.text_en: When you need both halves of a third-law pair, draw TWO separate free body diagrams — one for each body.
The book's FBD gets N, the force from the table on the book.
The table's FBD gets N prime, the force from the book on the table. Same magnitude, opposite direction, different body.
Now each FBD is consistent, each gives its own equation, and Newton's third law is respected.

STATE_1.description: State the FBD principle: isolate exactly one body, show every force on that body, ignore all others.

STATE_2.description: Isolate the chosen body. Replace each removed contact/connection with one labelled force arrow.

STATE_3.description: Apply Newton's second law: write sum(Fx) and sum(Fy). Substitute and solve.

STATE_4.description: Demonstrate on a book on a table: mg down, N up, sum(Fy) = 0 -> N = mg.

STATE_5.description: Draw incline FBD: mg vertical, N perpendicular to surface, f along surface. Keep the three directions correct.

STATE_6.description: Use the six-force checklist (mg, N, f, T, kx, hinge) to confirm no force is missing.

primitives.text: Step 1: Identify the body of interest in the problem.
Step 2: State the FBD principle — isolate one body, show every force on it.
Step 3: Ignore everything except forces acting on this one body.
+1 for stating the principle
Step 4: Redraw the body alone — detach it from neighbours.
Step 5: Replace each removed contact with an arrow (label it).
Step 6: Add mg at the centre of mass pointing down.
+1 for isolation + arrows
Step 7: Choose axes (usually x along motion, y perpendicular).
Step 8: Write sum(Fx) = m*ax and sum(Fy) = m*ay.
Step 9: Substitute known forces; solve for unknowns.
+1 for Newton's law application
Step 10: Example — book of mass 0.5 kg on a table.
Step 11: FBD: mg = 0.5 * 9.8 = 4.9 N down; N up.
Step 12: sum(Fy) = N - mg = 0 -> N = 4.9 N.
+1 for worked example
Step 13: On an incline at angle theta, mg stays vertical.
Step 14: N is perpendicular to the incline. f is along the incline, opposing motion.
Step 15: Choose axes along and perpendicular to the incline for easier equations.
+1 for incline FBD
Step 16: Run the six-force checklist before solving.
Step 17: Verify mg, N, f, T, kx, and hinge (H, V) are each shown if applicable.
Step 18: A missing force is the most common cause of wrong answers. Check twice.
+1 for complete checklist

shortcuts.formula: n bodies -> n FBDs -> n Newton equations
shortcuts.use_when: Any multi-body problem. Never merge two bodies into a single FBD unless treating them as one rigid sub-system with no internal slip.

shortcuts.rule: mg lives at the centre of mass
shortcuts.formula: Draw mg from CM straight down — regardless of support or contact point

shortcuts.rule: Each removed contact becomes ONE N (and optionally one f)
shortcuts.formula: surface contact -> N perp to surface; rough contact -> add f along surface
shortcuts.use_when: Scanning the problem for what arrows to draw.

shortcuts.rule: Third-law pairs live on DIFFERENT FBDs
shortcuts.formula: F_AB on A's FBD, F_BA on B's FBD — never both on one diagram

shortcuts.rule: Pick axes that align with motion or constraint
shortcuts.formula: Incline -> axes along/perp to incline. Circular -> radial/tangential. Generic -> x horizontal, y vertical.
shortcuts.use_when: Deciding how to resolve forces before writing equations.

shortcuts.rule: Non-inertial frame adds a pseudo force -m*a on every body
shortcuts.use_when: Working inside an accelerating lift/truck/rotating frame. In the ground frame, no pseudo force is needed.

edge_cases.title: String passing over a pulley
edge_cases.trap: Students draw different tensions on either side of a massless frictionless pulley.
edge_cases.resolution: For an ideal pulley, tension is the same throughout the string. One label T for both sides. Each mass's FBD shows the same T.

edge_cases.title: Apparent weight in an accelerating lift
edge_cases.trap: Students write N = mg instead of accounting for the lift's acceleration.
edge_cases.resolution: FBD of the person has N up and mg down. Apply sum(Fy) = ma_lift. N = m(g + a) when accelerating up, m(g - a) when accelerating down.

edge_cases.title: Block on an incline — which axes to pick?
edge_cases.trap: Students use horizontal/vertical axes, then must resolve N at an awkward angle.
edge_cases.resolution: Take x along the incline (down-slope positive), y perpendicular to incline. Then N has no x component, and mg splits as mg*sin(theta) along x and mg*cos(theta) along -y.

edge_cases.title: Two blocks pushed together on a frictionless floor
edge_cases.trap: Students forget the internal contact force when doing individual FBDs.
edge_cases.resolution: FBD of block 1: F_applied and contact force N_12 from block 2. FBD of block 2: contact force N_21 from block 1. N_12 and N_21 are a third-law pair.

edge_cases.title: Rigid body rotating about a pivot
edge_cases.trap: Students apply sum(F) = 0 when the body is actually rotating.
edge_cases.resolution: Add sum(tau) = I*alpha at the pivot. Force equations give the pivot reaction; torque equation gives the angular acceleration.
